title: National Liberation Struggle in Kazakhstan at the Beginning of the 20th Century: the Alash Movement
overview: Lecture 6 covers the rise of Kazakh national consciousness from the Karkaraly Petition of 1905 through the founding of the Alash Party (July 1917), the Alash Autonomy (5–13 December 1917) with its government Alash-Orda, the parallel Kokand (Turkestan) Autonomy, the 1916 National Liberation Uprising, the February and October Revolutions of 1917, and the eventual liquidation of the Alash project by the Bolsheviks. Alash leaders – Bukeikhanov, Baitursynov, Dulatov, Tynyshpaev and others – built the intellectual foundation of modern Kazakh statehood; most were repressed in 1937–1938 but are now honored as national heroes.

coreConcepts.definition: Main Kazakh socio-political newspaper (1913–1918, Orenburg); editor Ahmet Baitursynov. Platform of the future Alash party.
coreConcepts.example: From 1911–1915 the Kazakh cultural-educational magazine 'Aiqap' (Troitsk) was published with active participation by Zhihansha Seydalin, Mukhamedzhan Seralin and Yerkem Alimuly.

coreConcepts.definition: Kazakh national-democratic party founded at the First All-Kazakh Congress in July 1917 in Orenburg. Full program in 10 points published in 'Qazaq' on 1 November 1917.
coreConcepts.example: Programme covered state structure, local freedom, fundamental law, religion, courts, protection of the people, taxes, labor, science and education, and land. Opponent: pro-socialist 'Ush Zhuz' party led by Kulbai Togusov.

coreConcepts.definition: Government of the Alash Autonomy proclaimed at the Second All-Kazakh Congress in Orenburg on 5–13 December 1917. Chairman of the People's Council: Alikhan Bukeikhanov.
coreConcepts.example: Eastern centre at Semipalatinsk (Alash-kala) under Bukeikhanov; Western Alash-Orda at Zhympity under Zhansha Dosmukhamedov.

coreConcepts.definition: Petition from Kazakh bourgeoisie and intelligentsia at the Koyandy fair near Karkaraly demanding civil rights, end of resettlement, Kazakh-language office work, jury trials and Duma representation.
coreConcepts.example: Organized by A. Bukeikhanov, A. Baitursynov, Zh. Akbaev, T. Nurekenov and others.

coreConcepts.definition: All-Russian Constituent Assembly (1917) – elections in which Alash participated to defend Kazakh autonomy.
coreConcepts.example: Bolsheviks dispersed the Assembly in January 1918, removing the legal path to Alash autonomy.

coreConcepts.definition: Provisional Muslim government in Kokand, November 1917 – February 1918, parallel to Alash-Orda. Political leaders: Mustafa Shokay, Mukhamedzhan Tynyshpaev.
coreConcepts.example: Crushed by the Bolsheviks in February 1918 with great human casualties and destruction.

expandedNotes.heading: Socio-Economic Situation in Early 20th-Century Kazakhstan
expandedNotes.body: By the early 20th century Kazakhstan was a colony of the Russian Empire. Kazakhs had limited civil and political rights, were deprived of the vote, and could not fully participate in political life. LAND: Stolypin's agrarian policy accelerated mass peasant resettlement; large areas of Kazakh land went to settlers, impoverishing nomads. ECONOMY: agrarian periphery with minimal industry – livestock breeding (nomadic and semi-nomadic) remained the base. Forced sedentarization reduced productivity. The Orenburg–Tashkent railway primarily served settler and metropolitan interests. EDUCATION: limited – most Kazakhs got religious education in maktabs and madrasahs; the majority were illiterate by European standards. Russification opened Russian schools and reduced Kazakh-language ones. HEALTH: relatively developed but inaccessible to the indigenous population.

expandedNotes.heading: Karkaraly Petition (1905) and Duma Activity
expandedNotes.body: On 25 July 1905 at the Koyandy fair near Karkaraly, the Kazakh bourgeoisie and intelligentsia presented a petition to the Russian government with the following demands: stop resettlement from European Russia; separate spiritual administration for the Kazakhs; abolition of censorship; introduction of Kazakh-language office work; introduction of jury trials; Kazakh deputies in the State Duma; freedom to build mosques and madrasahs and bar missionary activity; freedom to open printing houses; stop withdrawal of land and return previously withdrawn lands. The petition – organized by A. Bukeikhanov, A. Baitursynov, Zh. Akbaev, T. Nurekenov – is treated by historians as the BEGINNING of the Alash movement. DUMA: 9 Kazakhs served. First Duma 1906: Akhmet Birimzhanov (Turgay), Alihan Bukeikhanov (Semey), Shaimerden Koshuylov (Akmola), Alpysbay Kalmenov (Oral), Salimgerey Zhantorin (Ufa), Baqytkerey Kulmanov (Astrakhan), Daut Noyan Tundutov (Astrakhan), Molda Tayynov (Zhetysu). Second Duma 1907: Tileuli Allabergenov (Syrdarya), Akhmet Birimzhanov (Turgay), Shaimerden Koshulov (Akmola), Baqytkerey Kulmanov (Astrakhan), Temirgali Nurekenov (Semey), Mukhamedzhan Tynyshpaev (Zhetysu), Bagytzhan Karataev (Oral). Karataev's speech in the 2nd Duma denounced the colonial agrarian policy. The 1st and 2nd Dumas were dissolved after a few months and Kazakhs were once again deprived of suffrage.

expandedNotes.heading: Cultural and Educational Activities
expandedNotes.body: PUBLICATIONS: Abay's poems (1909); 'Oyan, Qazaq!' ('Wake up, Kazakh!') by M. Dulatov (1909); 'Masa' by A. Baitursynov (1911); magazine 'Aiqap' (1911–1915, Troitsk); newspaper 'Qazaq' (1913–1918, Orenburg). Abay Kunanbaev's poems and 'Qara Sözder' ('Words of Edification') strongly shaped Kazakh public consciousness. GOALS of cultural-educational work: consolidate society around the idea of Alash; develop secular education; develop national literature; protect national interests; struggle for autonomy through political means.

expandedNotes.heading: 1916 Uprising: Position of the Intellectuals
expandedNotes.body: CAUSES: mass resettlement reducing Kazakh land holdings; high taxes; forced labor; limited economic opportunities; political oppression; restricted representation. The immediate catalyst was Nicholas II's decree of 25 June 1916 requisitioning the non-Russian population of Central Asia and Kazakhstan for rear work. LEADERSHIP: in Turgay – Abdiqappar Janbosynov and Amangeldy Imanov organized armed detachments; Alibi Zhangildin coordinated. In Zhetysu (Semirechye) – Tokash Bokin, Uzak Saurykov, others. POSITIONS: the national intelligentsia was DIVIDED. Moderates (Akhmet Baitursynov, Alikhan Bukeikhanov, Mukhamedzhan Tynyshpaev, Mirzhakyp Dulatov, Sh. Qudaiberdiuly) urged negotiation and feared mass casualties – they did not support armed uprising. Radicals (Turar Ryskulov, Saken Seyfullin, A. Zhangaeldin, S. Mendeshev) supported it as a justified step in the struggle for Kazakh rights. 'Qazaq' newspaper actively covered events. OUTCOMES: brutally suppressed; thousands sent to prisons or penal servitude; many Kazakhs fled to China. The uprising became a precondition for the political reconfiguration of Central Asia after February 1917.

expandedNotes.heading: Influence of the February Revolution (1917)
expandedNotes.body: The Provisional Government's overthrow of the monarchy democratized political life: freedom of speech and assembly let Kazakhs openly discuss and promote their ideas. Political and social organizations were created, including the Alash party. Kazakhs gained the opportunity to participate in the elections to the Constituent Assembly. Leaders A. Bukeikhanov, A. Baitursynov, M. Dulatov, M. Tynyshpaev sought constructive relations with the Provisional Government, aiming for autonomy and peaceful reforms. The result was rapid activation of the national movement and growth of political consciousness.

expandedNotes.heading: Alash Party Programme
expandedNotes.body: STATE STRUCTURE: proclamation of Kazakhstan's autonomy within a federative democratic Russia; establishment of its own governance (legislative, executive, judicial branches); separation of powers. LAND: restoration and protection of traditional Kazakh lands; halt of resettlement policy; rational use and distribution. ECONOMY: national economic development; protection of household and farmer interests; trade and entrepreneurship. SOCIAL: improving living conditions; social reforms protecting workers' rights; social justice and equality. EDUCATION & CULTURE: national education; publishing textbooks in Kazakh; preservation of culture, literature, art. RELIGION: freedom of religion; separation of religion and state. LEGAL SYSTEM: fair legal system based on democratic principles; protection of rights and freedoms; independent judiciary.

expandedNotes.heading: Alash Autonomy (5–13 December 1917)
expandedNotes.body: The Second All-Kazakh Congress in Orenburg on 5–13 December 1917 proclaimed Alash Autonomy and established Alash-Orda as its government. Chair of the People's Council: A. Bukeikhanov. EASTERN centre: Semipalatinsk (Alash-kala); WESTERN Alash-Orda (Zhympity): Zhansha Dosmukhamedov. GOALS: autonomous Alash state within a federative Russia; national self-governance; land protection against resettlement; opening schools and printing Kazakh-language books; fair taxation; supporting agriculture. The Bolsheviks did not recognize the autonomy. During the Civil War (1918–1920) Alash-Orda maneuvered between the Whites (Kolchak, Dutov) and the Reds; many alashevtsy transferred to Soviet side after the 1919 amnesty. On 5 March 1920 VTsIK decreed the dissolution of Alash-Orda.

expandedNotes.heading: Kokand (Turkestan) Autonomy
expandedNotes.body: Parallel autonomy proclaimed in Kokand in November 1917. POLITICAL LEADERS: Mukhamedzhan Tynyshpaev, Mustafa Shokay. GOALS: autonomy for Turkic peoples; protection of Muslim rights and cultural heritage; national self-governance. ACTIVITIES: provisional government formed November 1917; local administrative structures; emphasis on Islamic education; support for local economy and trade. DISSOLUTION: in February 1918 the Bolsheviks forcibly dismantled the Kokand autonomy, causing significant casualties. COMPARISON WITH ALASH: both aimed at self-governing national states within federative Russia; Alash focused on Kazakh land rights and secular national revival, while Kokand emphasized Islamic traditions and pan-Turkic heritage. Both were dismantled by the Bolsheviks.

expandedNotes.heading: Impact of the October Revolution (1917)
expandedNotes.body: Soviet authority established between October 1917 and March 1918, unevenly across regions. Peaceful establishment in Perovsk (now Kyzylorda – the earliest Soviet centre), most of Syrdarya, Akmola regions and the Bukey Horde. Established by force of arms in Turgay, Ural, Semipalatinsk and Semirechensk regions. Opponents of Soviet rule were Cossack populations, Socialist-Revolutionaries, Mensheviks, and Alash. Soviet policy nationalized land and industry, fundamentally changing the economy. Land and industrial redistribution disrupted traditional Kazakh livelihoods. Despite an amnesty, repressions began against Alash-Orda leaders and national intelligentsia. Long-term: Soviet rule launched mass socio-economic transformations and laid the groundwork for the catastrophe of collectivization (Lecture 7).

expandedNotes.heading: Repression and Legacy
expandedNotes.body: After the Bolsheviks consolidated power, many leaders of the Alash movement were repressed. Despite suppression, Alash ideas and achievements had a lasting impact on subsequent national consciousness and culture. In independent Kazakhstan, Alash leaders are honored as national heroes. Their legacy includes the Karkaraly Petition, Duma participation, publication of 'Qazaq', and the functioning of the Alash Party, Alash Autonomy, and Alash-Orda – concrete steps toward political and administrative self-governance.

keyScholars.name: Alikhan Bukeikhanov
keyScholars.contribution: Leader of the Alash movement; deputy in the 1st State Duma (1906); chair of Alash-Orda People's Council. Executed in 1937.

keyScholars.name: Akhmet Baitursynov
keyScholars.contribution: Linguist and editor of 'Qazaq' (1913–1918); reformer of the Kazakh alphabet ('töte zhazu'); author of 'Masa' (1911). Executed in 1937.

keyScholars.name: Mirzhakyp Dulatov
keyScholars.contribution: Poet; author of 'Oyan, Qazaq!' (1909) – the national-awakening manifesto.

keyScholars.name: Mukhamedzhan Tynyshpaev
keyScholars.contribution: First Kazakh railway engineer; deputy in the 2nd State Duma (1907); leader of Kokand Autonomy. Executed in 1937.

keyScholars.name: Magzhan Zhumabaev
keyScholars.contribution: Poet and ideologue of Turkism within Alash. Executed in 1938.

keyScholars.name: Bakhytzhan Karataev
keyScholars.contribution: Deputy in the 2nd State Duma; spoke from the Duma rostrum against colonial agrarian policy.

keyScholars.name: Mustafa Shokay
keyScholars.contribution: Leader of Kokand (Turkestan) Autonomy; later émigré intellectual who continued the Turkestan project abroad.

keyScholars.name: Abay Kunanbaev
keyScholars.contribution: Foundational poet-philosopher; his poems (collected 1909) and 'Qara Sözder' shaped Alash thought.

keyScholars.name: Turar Ryskulov
keyScholars.contribution: Radical leader during 1916; later Deputy Chairman of the RSFSR Council of People's Commissars; sent the 1933 letter to Stalin on the Kazakh famine. Repressed.

comparativeTables.title: Key Publications of the Alash Era
rows: Mukhamedzhan Seralin, Zhihansha Seydalin, Yerkem Alimuly (Troitsk)

realWorldExamples: The newspaper 'Qazaq' (1913–1918) has been digitized by the National Library of Kazakhstan and is now read as a foundational document of national consciousness.
Alikhan Bukeikhanov's monument stands in Semey (former Semipalatinsk) – the historical centre of Alash-Orda.
Mustafa Shokay's grave in Nogent-sur-Marne (France) became a place of pilgrimage after independence.
Streets, schools and universities across Kazakhstan are now named after Bukeikhanov, Baitursynov, Dulatov, Tynyshpaev and Zhumabaev.
On 28 November Kazakhstan officially commemorates the Day of the Alash movement.

timeline.event: Karkaraly Petition presented at the Koyandy fair
First Russian State Duma – 8 Kazakh deputies
Second State Duma – 7 Kazakh deputies
Publication of M. Dulatov's 'Oyan, Qazaq!' and Abay's collected poems
A. Baitursynov's 'Masa'; magazine 'Aiqap' begins
Newspaper 'Qazaq' founded in Orenburg
Nicholas II's decree on requisition of inorodtsy
February Revolution; democratization opens space for the Kazakh movement
First All-Kazakh Congress; founding of the Alash Party in Orenburg
Alash Party programme published in 'Qazaq'
Provisional government of Kokand (Turkestan) Autonomy formed
Second All-Kazakh Congress proclaims Alash Autonomy and Alash-Orda
Bolsheviks dismantle Kokand Autonomy
Turkestan ASSR proclaimed (capital Tashkent)
Soviet amnesty; many Alash leaders move to Soviet side
VTsIK dissolves Alash-Orda
Kyrgyz (Kazakh) ASSR formed, capital Orenburg
Republic renamed Kazakh ASSR; capital moved to Kyzylorda
Capital moved to Alma-Ata
Most Alash leaders repressed and executed (Bukeikhanov, Baitursynov, Tynyshpaev, Zhumabaev, Ryskulov)

mustMemorize: July 1917 – Alash Party founded (1st All-Kazakh Congress, Orenburg)
5–13 December 1917 – Alash Autonomy proclaimed (2nd Congress); Alash-Orda, chair A. Bukeikhanov
Kokand (Turkestan) Autonomy – Nov 1917 – Feb 1918; M. Shokay, M. Tynyshpaev
Alash programme = 10 points in 'Qazaq' on 1 November 1917
Eastern Alash-Orda – Semipalatinsk (Bukeikhanov); Western – Zhympity (Zh. Dosmukhamedov)
26 August 1920 – Kyrgyz (Kazakh) ASSR formed, capital Orenburg
cheatSheet:
1905 Karkaraly → 1906/1907 Duma → 1913 'Qazaq' → Jul 1917 Alash Party → Dec 1917 Alash-Orda + Kokand Autonomy → 1918–1920 Civil War → 1920 KazASSR
Leaders: Bukeikhanov + Baitursynov + Dulatov + Tynyshpaev + Zhumabaev + Shokay (Kokand)
Programme = 10 points; rivals = 'Ush Zhuz' (pro-Soviet, Kulbai Togusov)
mnemonics: B-B-D-T-Zh-Sh – Bukeikhanov, Baitursynov, Dulatov, Tynyshpaev, Zhumabaev, Shokay (6 founding fathers)
'Oyan, Qazaq!' (1909) = 'Wake up, Kazakh!' – Dulatov's call to action
Alash = LAND focus; Kokand = ISLAM focus
5/12/17 → 5/3/20 – Alash-Orda was born and died on the 5th
